1: The communal call to return -- 'Come, let us return to the LORD. He has torn us, but he will heal us' -- anticipates Jesus's invitation: 'Come to me, all who labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest' (Matthew 11:28). The acknowledgment that YHWH both wounds and heals, tears and binds up, is fulfilled in Christ who bears the wound so that the healing comes to his people. On the cross, Christ is the one torn and wounded; in the resurrection, the healing that Hosea's Israel hopes for is enacted in history. 'He himself bore our sins in his body on the tree, that we might die to sin and live to righteousness. By his wounds you have been healed' (1 Peter 2:24).
2: The third-day resurrection language -- 'After two days he will revive us; on the third day he will raise us up' -- is one of the OT's most significant anticipations of Christ's resurrection. Paul's summary of the gospel in 1 Corinthians 15:4 states that Christ 'was raised on the third day in accordance with the Scriptures' -- and Hosea 6:2 is among the Scriptures the NT tradition draws on (alongside Genesis 22:4; Exodus 19:11; Joshua 1:11; 2 Kings 20:5; Jonah 1:17). The 'we' of Hosea's corporate resurrection hope is realized in Christ's individual resurrection that guarantees the resurrection of all who are in him: 'Christ has been raised from the dead, the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep' (1 Corinthians 15:20).
3: The call to press on to know the LORD -- his appearing as certain as the dawn, his coming like the rain -- describes the knowledge of God that Christ makes accessible. 'No one knows the Father except the Son and anyone to whom the Son chooses to reveal him' (Matthew 11:27). The dawn-certainty of YHWH's appearing finds its antitype in the resurrection dawn: 'early on the first day of the week, while it was still dark, Mary Magdalene came to the tomb' (John 20:1) -- the first light of Easter morning is the dawn of the new creation that Hosea's metaphor anticipates. The latter rain (maqosh) that refreshes the earth is a type of the Spirit poured out in Pentecost (Acts 2:17-18: 'I will pour out my Spirit on all flesh').
4: YHWH's exasperated question -- 'What shall I do with you, Ephraim? What shall I do with you, Judah?' -- is the expression of love frustrated by inconsistency. The morning cloud and early dew of Israel's love points to the contrast with Christ's love, which is anything but transient: 'Having loved his own who were in the world, he loved them to the end' (John 13:1). Where Israel's love evaporates before noon, Christ's endures through death. 'Who shall separate us from the love of Christ?' (Romans 8:35). The fleeting love that Hosea mourns finds its answer in the permanent, cross-proven love of Christ that neither morning cloud nor storm can dissipate.
5: YHWH having 'hewn them down by the prophets, slain them by the words of my mouth' is the divine word functioning as both judgment and revelation. Christ is the Word who both judges and saves: 'The word that I have spoken will judge him on the last day' (John 12:48) and 'the words that I have spoken to you are spirit and life' (John 6:63). The same word is both life-giving and slaying-of-the-old-self. Paul describes this as the living and active word: 'the word of God is living and active, sharper than any two-edged sword, piercing to the division of soul and of spirit' (Hebrews 4:12). Christ as the living Word is YHWH's prophet-ministry in person.
6: This is the most directly quoted verse in Hosea for Christ's own teaching. Jesus cites it twice: 'Go and learn what this means: I desire mercy, and not sacrifice. For I came not to call the righteous, but sinners' (Matthew 9:13); 'And if you had known what this means, I desire mercy, and not sacrifice, you would not have condemned the guiltless' (Matthew 12:7). In both citations, Jesus uses Hosea's principle to defend covenant love over ritual performance. The knowledge of God (daath Elohim) that Hosea sets above burnt offerings is identified with mercy (chesed) in practice -- the lived covenant love that the religious leaders had subordinated to cultic correctness. Christ himself is the embodiment of this principle: 'the Son of Man came not to be served but to serve, and to give his life as a ransom for many' (Mark 10:45) -- the ultimate act of chesed rather than sacrifice performed for personal benefit.
7: Israel's transgression 'like Adam' -- repeating the Edenic covenant violation -- is the typological frame for Christ as the second Adam who does not repeat the failure. 'As in Adam all die, so also in Christ shall all be made alive' (1 Corinthians 15:22). Paul's Adam-Christ typology (Romans 5:12-21) is the NT's extended commentary on Hosea 6:7: Adam transgressed the covenant; Christ obeyed where Adam disobeyed; the faithlessness of the one is answered by the faithfulness of the other. 'For as by the one man's disobedience the many were made sinners, so by the one man's obedience the many will be made righteous' (Romans 5:19).
8: Gilead stained with blood -- the city of evildoers whose iniquity is visible -- stands in contrast to Christ's blood which is the only blood that cleanses rather than stains. 'The blood of Jesus his Son cleanses us from all sin' (1 John 1:7). The blood that Gilead's violence produces is the blood that cries out for justice; the blood of Christ cries out for mercy: 'you have come to... Jesus, the mediator of a new covenant, and to the sprinkled blood that speaks a better word than the blood of Abel' (Hebrews 12:22, 24). The blood-stained city of Gilead is the antitype of the blood-cleansed people of the new covenant.
9: The bands of priests who murder on the road to Shechem -- spiritual leaders become predators -- are the antitype of Christ the Good Shepherd who lays down his life for the sheep rather than preying on them: 'The thief comes only to steal and kill and destroy. I came that they may have life and have it abundantly' (John 10:10). The road to Shechem was the road of covenant assembly; Christ makes the road of covenant encounter the road of abundant life rather than ambush. The murderous priesthood of Hosea is replaced by the High Priest who intercedes rather than preys: 'he always lives to make intercession for them' (Hebrews 7:25).
10: The 'horrible thing' seen in Israel -- Ephraim's prostitution, Israel's defilement -- is the condition that Christ comes to reverse through the cleansing of the new covenant. 'Christ loved the church and gave himself up for her, that he might sanctify her, having cleansed her by the washing of water with the word, so that he might present the church to himself in splendor, without spot or wrinkle or any such thing, that she might be holy and without blemish' (Ephesians 5:25-27). The defilement that Hosea identifies as Israel's condition is the condition Christ's sacrifice addresses: the bride who was defiled by prostitution is made clean by the bridegroom's self-giving.
11: Judah's appointed harvest -- ambiguous between judgment and restoration -- finds its resolution in Christ who is both the judge who reaps judgment (Revelation 14:15-16) and the one who makes restoration possible. 'Do you not say, There are yet four months, then comes the harvest? Look, I tell you, lift up your eyes, and see that the fields are white for harvest' (John 4:35). The harvest that Hosea leaves ambiguous becomes, in Christ's ministry, the harvest of the nations coming into the kingdom -- the ingathering that the restored fortunes of God's people make possible. 'The harvest is plentiful, but the laborers are few; therefore pray earnestly to the Lord of the harvest to send out laborers into his harvest' (Matthew 9:37-38).
